title_en: Salah: Conditions, Pillars, and Obligations of Prayer
content_en:
Salah (prayer) is the second pillar of Islam and the first act of worship for which a person will be held accountable on the Day of Judgment. The Prophet (peace be upon him) said: 'The first matter that the servant will be held accountable for on the Day of Resurrection is the prayer. If it is correct, the rest of his deeds will be correct; if it is corrupt, all of the rest of his deeds will be corrupt' (at-Tabarani).

In Zad al-Mustaqni', al-Hajjawi outlines the conditions (shurut) for the validity of prayer. These are: Islam, sound intellect, reaching the age of discernment (tamyiz), removing ritual impurity (hadath), removing physical impurity (najasah) from the body, clothing, and place of prayer, covering the 'awrah, facing the qiblah, entering the prayer time, and intention (niyyah).

The pillars (arkan) of prayer number fourteen in the Hanbali school: the standing posture (qiyam) when able, the opening takbir, recitation of Surah al-Fatihah, bowing (ruku') with tranquility (tuma'ninah), rising from ruku', prostration (sujud) on seven body parts with tranquility, sitting between the two prostrations with tranquility, the final tashahhud, sitting for the final tashahhud, invoking blessings on the Prophet in the final sitting, the two salams, and maintaining the proper sequence (tartib) throughout.

The obligations (wajibat) of prayer, distinct from pillars in that their omission may be compensated by the prostration of forgetfulness (sujud as-sahw), number eight: all the takbirat other than the opening one, saying 'Sami'a Allahu liman hamidah' (for the imam and one praying alone), saying 'Rabbana wa laka al-hamd,' saying 'Subhana Rabbiyal-'Azim' once in ruku', saying 'Subhana Rabbiyal-A'la' once in sujud, saying 'Rabbi ighfir li' between the two prostrations, the first tashahhud, and sitting for the first tashahhud.

The Hanbali school distinguishes carefully between pillars, obligations, and sunnahs (recommended acts). Omitting a pillar intentionally or forgetfully without completing it makes the prayer invalid. Omitting an obligation intentionally makes the prayer invalid; if done forgetfully, it is compensated by sujud as-sahw. Omitting a sunnah does not invalidate the prayer but diminishes its reward.

The times of the five daily prayers are established by Quranic verses and prophetic hadith. Fajr runs from true dawn until sunrise. Dhuhr begins after the sun passes its zenith and ends when the shadow of every object equals its length plus its noon shadow. 'Asr begins at that point and extends until sunset (with the preferred time ending when shadows double in length). Maghrib begins at sunset and extends until the red twilight disappears. 'Isha begins at that point and extends until midnight, though delay beyond midnight is makruh.

The Friday prayer (Jumu'ah) replaces Dhuhr for free, sane, adult, resident males. It requires a minimum of forty qualified attendees in the Hanbali school, two khutbahs before the prayer, and congregation (jama'ah). The two khutbahs must include praise of Allah, blessing upon the Prophet, exhortation to taqwa, and a Quranic verse.
